Skip to contents
roxyassert 0.0.0.9000
  • Reference
    • Getting started with roxyassert

roxyassert

Write the contract once — in your roxygen2 docs — and generate the runtime checks.

roxyassert reads structured type annotations in your roxygen2 documentation and, at document() time, generates per-function assertion helpers that validate a function’s arguments and its return value. The generated checks are plain calls to the assert package, written to a committed source file you can read and debug.

The point: you already describe each parameter’s type and each return shape in your @param / @return docs. Today you also hand-write a separate validation function that repeats all of it — and the two drift. roxyassert makes the documentation the single source of truth: the docs render for humans and generate the assertions, so they can never disagree.

How it works 

  R/*.R  (your code + typed roxygen tags)
     |
     |   devtools::document()   <- roxyassert runs here, as a roclet
     v
  R/contracts-generated.R       <- generated assert_args_* / assert_return_* helpers
     |
     |   you call them inside your functions, and commit the file
     v
  runtime validation via the `assert` package

roxyassert is a roxygen2 roclet: it runs in the documentation step (the same one that writes NAMESPACE and man/*.Rd), never during R CMD build/check. It parses tag text only — it never executes your code — and writes one file you commit alongside the rest of your generated artifacts.

Layering:

Layer Package Role
Vocabulary assert The runtime primitives (assert_scalar_character(), assert_data_table(), …).
Generator roxyassert Parses typed docs -> emits helpers that call assert.

assert has no dependency on roxyassert and stays lightweight; roxyassert depends on assert + roxygen2.

Installation 

# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("dereckscompany/roxyassert")

Setup (one line)

Register the roclet in your package’s DESCRIPTION:

Roxygen: list(markdown = TRUE, roclets = c("namespace", "rd", "roxyassert::contract_roclet"))

That’s the entire integration. Now devtools::document() runs roxyassert alongside the built-in roclets and (re)writes R/contracts-generated.R.

Quick example 

#' Submit an order.
#'
#' @param symbol (scalar<character>) normalised `BASE/QUOTE` pair.
#' @param side (enum<character, ORDER_SIDE>) order side.
#' @param quantity (scalar<numeric>) order size in the base asset.
#' @param price_limit (scalar<numeric>?) limit price; `NULL` for market orders.
#' @return (data.table) the accepted order:
#' - **order_id** (character): exchange order id.
#' - **status** (character): order status.
#' - **quantity** (numeric): accepted size.
#' - **datetime** (POSIXct): acceptance time.
#' @export
submit_order <- function(symbol, side, quantity, price_limit = NULL) {
  assert_args_submit_order(symbol, side, quantity, price_limit)   # generated
  result <- ...
  return(assert_return_submit_order(result))                      # generated
}

After document(), R/contracts-generated.R contains (illustrative):

# Generated by roxyassert -- do not edit by hand.

assert_args_submit_order <- function(symbol, side, quantity, price_limit) {
  assert::assert_scalar_character(symbol)
  assert::assert_value_in_set(side, ORDER_SIDE)
  assert::assert_scalar_numeric(quantity)
  if (!is.null(price_limit)) assert::assert_scalar_numeric(price_limit)
  invisible(NULL)
}

assert_return_submit_order <- function(value) {
  assert::assert_data_table(value)
  assert::assert_column_types(value, "character", c("order_id", "status"))
  assert::assert_column_types(value, "numeric", "quantity")
  assert::assert_column_types(value, "POSIXct", "datetime")
  return(value)   # returned untouched, so you can wrap it in return()/invisible()
}

The annotation grammar

A type annotation is a parenthesised token at the start of a @param or @return description. Plain prose @param/@return tags without a leading (...) token are ignored — so adoption is incremental and opt-in.

Scalars and vectors

Annotation Meaning
(scalar<character>) length-1 character
(scalar<numeric>) length-1 numeric
(vector<numeric>) numeric of any length (>= 1)
(vector<character>[10]) character of exactly 10 elements
(vector<numeric>[1..10]) numeric of length 1 to 10
(vector<integer>[2..]) integer of length at least 2

scalar<T> is shorthand for vector<T>[1].

Base types

character, numeric, integer, logical, complex, factor, list, function, POSIXct, Date.

Modifiers

Annotation Meaning
(scalar<numeric>?) nullable / optional — NULL or a missing argument passes; otherwise the inner type is enforced.
(enum<character, ORDER_SIDE>) value(s) must be members of the set/constant ORDER_SIDE (any object resolving to a vector of allowed values, e.g. a core constant).
(R6<Engine>) an R6 instance inheriting class Engine. Use this for builder methods that return invisible(self).

data.tables / data.frames

Annotate the tag (data.table) (or (data.frame)), then declare the columns as a markdown bullet list in this exact form:

#' @return (data.table) open orders:
#' - **order_id** (character): exchange order id.
#' - **price** (numeric): limit price.
#' - **datetime** (POSIXct): submission time.

Each bullet - **name** (type): description becomes a column assertion. Column type uses the base types above. Because every column is checked for an atomic type, list-columns are rejected by construction — a nested/list column fails its declared type.

The same bullet syntax is the human-readable column documentation you already write, so the docs and the generated schema come from one place.

Generated functions — conventions

  • Two helpers, each optional. assert_args_<fn> is emitted only if the function has at least one typed @param; assert_return_<fn> only if @return carries a parseable type. Functions with no typed tags get nothing.
  • Explicit arguments. assert_args_<fn>(arg1, arg2, ...) takes the documented parameters by name, in declaration order — you call it explicitly so the contract is visible at the call site. (If you change a function’s arguments, re-run document() and update the call.)
  • Returns are passed through. assert_return_<fn>(value) validates and returns value unchanged, so you can write return(assert_return_fn(x)) or keep your own invisible().
  • invisible() is irrelevant. Whether a function returns visibly or invisibly is a print concern, not a contract — generation depends solely on a typed @return. A @return invisible(self) written as prose is skipped; annotate @return (R6<Engine>) to assert it.
  • R6 methods are scoped by class. A method submit on class Engine generates assert_args_Engine__submit (double-underscore separator), so two classes sharing a method name — or a method and a free function with the same name — never collide.
  • Collision-safe. If two generated names would ever clash, generation aborts with a clear error rather than silently overwriting.
  • Internal, undocumented, committed. Generated helpers are not exported and carry no .Rd; they live in a single R/contracts-generated.R (with a do-not-edit banner) that you commit like NAMESPACE. Each package generates and uses its own.

Supported targets

  • Plain functions: yes
  • R6 class methods: yes
  • S4 / Reference classes: not yet.

Configuration

Defaults need no config. Basic options (active roclets, output) live in the DESCRIPTION Roxygen: field. Richer settings (generated-file path, strictness) are read from an optional roxyassert.yml if present; absent, sensible defaults apply.

Why not put the type in the code instead?

Packages like typed declare types inside the function body with a custom operator. That works and checks at runtime, but it changes how every function is written. roxyassert keeps your R code exactly as it is and treats the documentation you already write as the contract.

Status

Early development — the annotation grammar and generation conventions documented here are the design under active implementation.
